MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): The paper presents a new Polish evaluation corpus but reports no comparisons to prior datasets or baseline systems (none mentioned), so it fails to position itself relative to existing STS/entailment work such as SemEval STS tasks or the SemEval/2014 CDM evaluations. While inter-annotator metrics are reported and related work is cited, there are no empirical baseline results or fairness controls (same models/data/metrics) to judge dataset difficulty or utility. From an experimental-design perspective this is inadequate: at minimum simple baselines and references to comparable benchmarks should have been included to contextualize the contribution. | B(1): The paper presents a new Polish CDS evaluation corpus but reports no empirical comparisons to existing baselines or prior evaluation suites (e.g., SICK, SemEval STS benchmarks or SemEval compositional evaluation tasks). While related work is cited adequately, the manuscript lacks experiments showing how existing models or benchmarks perform on the new dataset and does not analyze when prior methods perform comparably or better. From the perspective of a resources/impact specialist, this omission prevents assessing the dataset’s practical value for advancing CDS research or how it differs empirically from prior resources.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a new Polish evaluation corpus but reports no comparisons to prior datasets or baseline systems (none mentioned), so it fails to position itself relative to existing STS/entailment work such as SemEval STS tasks or the SemEval/2014 CDM evaluations. While inter-annotator metrics are reported and related work is cited, there are no empirical baseline results or fairness controls (same models/data/metrics) to judge dataset difficulty or utility. From an experimental-design perspective this is inadequate: at minimum simple baselines and references to comparable benchmarks should have been included to contextualize the contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a new Polish CDS evaluation corpus but reports no empirical comparisons to existing baselines or prior evaluation suites (e.g., SICK, SemEval STS benchmarks or SemEval compositional evaluation tasks). While related work is cited adequately, the manuscript lacks experiments showing how existing models or benchmarks perform on the new dataset and does not analyze when prior methods perform comparably or better. From the perspective of a resources/impact specialist, this omission prevents assessing the dataset’s practical value for advancing CDS research or how it differs empirically from prior resources.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical progression from motivation through method to results, and the main contributions (a procedure adapted from SICK and a 10K Polish evaluation corpus) are stated early and clearly. Notation and methodological descriptions are generally clear for a dataset paper, and reported agreement statistics (Fleiss’ κ, Krippendorff’s α) are presented to support data quality. A minor weakness is that some presentation details are under-specified in the summary (e.g., whether figures/tables and examples effectively illustrate transformation rules and error cases, and whether code/preprocessing will be released), but overall the writing is clear and readable. | B(4): The summary indicates the paper is well-structured (procedure description, corpus construction, annotation and agreement statistics) and states its contributions early (Polish CDS dataset and adaptation of SICK procedures). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and clear enough for reproduction, and examples/metrics (κ and α values, dataset size) are reported to support claims. A few minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., explicit mention of figures/tables and code availability is unclear), but overall the writing quality is good and the flow logical.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical progression from motivation through method to results, and the main contributions (a procedure adapted from SICK and a 10K Polish evaluation corpus) are stated early and clearly. A minor weakness is that some presentation details are under-specified in the summary (e.g., whether figures/tables and examples effectively illustrate transformation rules and error cases, and whether code/preprocessing will be released), but overall the writing is clear and readable.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates the paper is well-structured (procedure description, corpus construction, annotation and agreement statistics) and states its contributions early (Polish CDS dataset and adaptation of SICK procedures). APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work addresses a core NLP evaluation need—building a sizeable, carefully annotated Polish test corpus for semantic relatedness and entailment—clearly within the scope of top-tier NLP venues. The methodology adapts the well-known SICK pipeline, reports reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78), and includes language-specific dependency transformations and human verification, which will interest the venue’s audience. Remaining concerns that keep this from a perfect fit are the absence of reported baselines or downstream evaluations and missing details about code/release and exact annotation/adjudication procedures needed for full reproducibility. | B(4): This paper fits the scope of a top NLP venue because it produces a substantial (10K-pair) Polish evaluation resource aimed explicitly at compositional distributional semantics, with careful adaptation of SICK-style methodology and reasonably high inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78). From my perspective as a semantic resources specialist it addresses the right audience and standards by tackling language-specific transformation rules and human verification for dependency-parsing errors, but the submission appears to lack accompanying baselines, released code, or explicit benchmarking that would increase its practical impact and adoption. With those additions or clearer release plans the work would be an excellent fit; as-is it is a strong but not perfect match for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses a core NLP evaluation need—building a sizeable, carefully annotated Polish test corpus for semantic relatedness and entailment—clearly within the scope of top-tier NLP venues. The methodology adapts the well-known SICK pipeline, reports reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78), and includes language-specific dependency transformations and human verification, which will interest the venue’s audience. Remaining concerns that keep this from a perfect fit are the absence of reported baselines or downstream evaluations and missing details about code/release and exact annotation/adjudication procedures needed for full reproducibility.

From my perspective as a semantic resources specialist it addresses the right audience and standards by tackling language-specific transformation rules and human verification for dependency-parsing errors, but the submission appears to lack accompanying baselines, released code, or explicit benchmarking that would increase its practical impact and adoption. With those additions or clearer release plans the work would be an excellent fit; as-is it is a strong but not perfect match for a top-tier venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a substantial 10K-pair Polish evaluation corpus with reasonable IAA reporting (κ=0.732, α=0.78) and discusses key language-specific limitations, which supports the main dataset claims. However, it lacks experiments applying models or baselines on the new corpus, provides no ablations/sensitivity analyses of transformation rules or parser error impact, and does not clearly state code, transformation rules, seeds, or full annotation/adjudication artefacts needed for reproducibility. Related work is reported as adequate and limitations are acknowledged, but the absence of model evaluations and detailed release documentation leaves important questions about dataset reliability and downstream utility unanswered. | B(3): The paper provides a solid dataset construction procedure and reports reasonable annotation agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78), and situates itself against SICK and STS resources (e.g., SemEval/SICK). However, no empirical baselines or downstream evaluations are presented to demonstrate the dataset’s utility for CDS models, and there are no ablations or sensitivity analyses of the automatic transformation rules or parser error impact. Related work coverage appears adequate, and limitations (language-specific transformations, parsing errors) are discussed, but reproducibility is weakened by lack of code or detailed transformation specifications in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a substantial 10K-pair Polish evaluation corpus with reasonable IAA reporting (κ=0.732, α=0.78) and discusses key language-specific limitations, which supports the main dataset claims. However, it lacks experiments applying models or baselines on the new corpus, provides no ablations/sensitivity analyses of transformation rules or parser error impact, and does not clearly state code, transformation rules, seeds, or full annotation/adjudication artefacts needed for reproducibility. Related work is reported as adequate and limitations are acknowledged, but the absence of model evaluations and detailed release documentation leaves important questions about dataset reliability and downstream utility unanswered.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a solid dataset construction procedure and reports reasonable annotation agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78), and situates itself against SICK and STS resources (e.g., SemEval/SICK). However, no empirical baselines or downstream evaluations are presented to demonstrate the dataset’s utility for CDS models, and there are no ablations or sensitivity analyses of the automatic transformation rules or parser error impact. Related work coverage appears adequate, and limitations (language-specific transformations, parsing errors) are discussed, but reproducibility is weakened by lack of code or detailed transformation specifications in the summary.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a useful resource-building procedure and a sizable Polish CDS evaluation set, but conceptually it largely adapts the established SICK/SemEval paradigm to a new language rather than proposing a fundamentally new method. The language-specific transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, and attention to Polish dependency-based generation are meaningful engineering contributions, yet they are incremental relative to prior STS and entailment corpora (e.g. SICK, SemEval tasks). From an experimental-methods perspective this would inform practitioners working on under-resourced languages but would not surprise experts as a novel theoretical advance. | B(3): The paper produces a substantial Polish evaluation corpus by adapting the SICK procedure and adding language-specific dependency-transformations, bidirectional pairing, and a 10K-pair scale—useful for under-resourced Polish CDS work. However, the overall approach follows well-established dataset construction practices for STS/entailment (e.g., SICK, SemEval STS) rather than introducing fundamentally new methods or theoretical perspectives. The reported annotation rigor and Polish-specific transformation rules do differentiate it practically, but the contribution is best characterized as an important, incremental resource rather than a novel methodological advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a useful resource-building procedure and a sizable Polish CDS evaluation set, but conceptually it largely adapts the established SICK/SemEval paradigm to a new language rather than proposing a fundamentally new method. The language-specific transformation rules, bidirectional entailment annotation, and attention to Polish dependency-based generation are meaningful engineering contributions, yet they are incremental relative to prior STS and entailment corpora (e.g. SICK, SemEval tasks). From an experimental-methods perspective this would inform practitioners working on under-resourced languages but would not surprise experts as a novel theoretical advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper produces a substantial Polish evaluation corpus by adapting the SICK procedure and adding language-specific dependency-transformations, bidirectional pairing, and a 10K-pair scale—useful for under-resourced Polish CDS work. However, the overall approach follows well-established dataset construction practices for STS/entailment (e.g., SICK, SemEval STS) rather than introducing fundamentally new methods or theoretical perspectives. The reported annotation rigor and Polish-specific transformation rules do differentiate it practically, but the contribution is best characterized as an important, incremental resource rather than a novel methodological advance.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper addresses an important practical gap—creating a Polish CDS evaluation set—by adapting a proven SICK-style protocol and producing a substantial 10K paired-corpus with reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78). From an experimental-methods perspective it is a useful resource that will enable evaluation of Polish semantic models, but without reported baselines, released code, or demonstration of downstream impact the work is more of an enabling dataset than a field-changing contribution. Its value is therefore moderate: likely to be used by researchers working on Polish semantics but not to substantially alter broader theoretical directions. | B(3): This paper fills an important gap by providing a sizable (10K pair) Polish evaluation corpus for compositional distributional semantics with solid annotation quality (Fleiss’ κ=0.732, Krippendorff’s α=0.78) and explicit Polish-specific transformation procedures. It is likely to be adopted by researchers working on Polish CDS and cross-lingual comparisons, but it is largely an adaptation of SICK rather than a conceptually novel resource and lacks reported baselines or released tooling, which limits immediate paradigm-shifting impact. The dataset opens useful research directions for language-specific transformation methods and evaluation in inflectional languages, yet its influence beyond the Polish/NLP-evaluation community will be moderate unless accompanied by benchmarks and code.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important practical gap—creating a Polish CDS evaluation set—by adapting a proven SICK-style protocol and producing a substantial 10K paired-corpus with reasonable inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.732, α=0.78). From an experimental-methods perspective it is a useful resource that will enable evaluation of Polish semantic models, but without reported baselines, released code, or demonstration of downstream impact the work is more of an enabling dataset than a field-changing contribution. Its value is therefore moderate: likely to be used by researchers working on Polish semantics but not to substantially alter broader theoretical directions.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fills an important gap by providing a sizable (10K pair) Polish evaluation corpus for compositional distributional semantics with solid annotation quality (Fleiss’ κ=0.732, Krippendorff’s α=0.78) and explicit Polish-specific transformation procedures. It is likely to be adopted by researchers working on Polish CDS and cross-lingual comparisons, but it is largely an adaptation of SICK rather than a conceptually novel resource and lacks reported baselines or released tooling, which limits immediate paradigm-shifting impact. The dataset opens useful research directions for language-specific transformation methods and evaluation in inflectional languages, yet its influence beyond the Polish/NLP-evaluation community will be moderate unless accompanied by benchmarks and code.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents a plausible procedure and supplies inter‑annotator agreement numbers (κ=0.732, α=0.78) supporting label quality, but important methodological details are missing for full technical validation. Key gaps include absence of released code or explicit transformation rules and parsing error rates (limiting reproducibility), lack of annotation qualification/adjudication details, and no baseline experiments demonstrating the dataset's utility for evaluating CDS models. These omissions raise concerns about reproducibility and whether the dataset enables robust downstream evaluation, so the contribution is promising but not fully validated. | B(4): The paper's main technical claims—that a 10K-pair Polish evaluation corpus was constructed following an adapted SICK procedure and that it attains reasonably high annotation reliability (Fleiss’ κ = 0.732 for entailment, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78 for relatedness)—are supported by the reported annotation protocol and agreement statistics. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the transformation + two-stage human verification pipeline is described and acknowledges parser-induced errors that are mitigated by manual correction. A notable methodological omission is the lack of downstream baselines or empirical validation demonstrating the dataset's utility for CDS models, which leaves a minor gap between dataset construction and evidence of practical usefulness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible procedure and supplies inter‑annotator agreement numbers (κ=0.732, α=0.78) supporting label quality, but important methodological details are missing for full technical validation. Key gaps include absence of released code or explicit transformation rules and parsing error rates (limiting reproducibility), lack of annotation qualification/adjudication details, and no baseline experiments demonstrating the dataset's utility for evaluating CDS models. These omissions raise concerns about reproducibility and whether the dataset enables robust downstream evaluation, so the contribution is promising but not fully validated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main technical claims—that a 10K-pair Polish evaluation corpus was constructed following an adapted SICK procedure and that it attains reasonably high annotation reliability (Fleiss’ κ = 0.732 for entailment, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78 for relatedness)—are supported by the reported annotation protocol and agreement statistics. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the transformation + two-stage human verification pipeline is described and acknowledges parser-induced errors that are mitigated by manual correction. A notable methodological omission is the lack of downstream baselines or empirical validation demonstrating the dataset's utility for CDS models, which leaves a minor gap between dataset construction and evidence of practical usefulness.

main_claims: The paper presents a procedure for building a Polish evaluation dataset for validating compositional distributional semantics (CDS) models, generally based on the design of the SICK corpus but modified for Polish-specific needs.
Using this procedure the authors build a Polish test corpus consisting of 10K sentence pairs, human-annotated for semantic relatedness and entailment (with entailment annotated in both directions).

The dataset is of relatively high quality, with reported inter-annotator agreement κ = 0.732 (Fleiss’ kappa) for entailment labels and α = 0.78 (Krippendorff’s alpha) for relatedness scores, and will be made publicly available upon publication.
_summary.methodology: Adapt the SICK corpus assembly steps for Polish by selecting images from the 8K ImageFlickr dataset, writing Polish seed sentences grouped thematically, applying automatic language-specific transformation rules on dependency structures, forming sentence pairs (including bidirectional pairs), and performing two-stage human annotation with leader verification and post-corrections.
datasets: Polish evaluation/test corpus (10K sentence pairs) [constructed by the authors]
SemEval-2012 Semantic Textual Similarity dataset (Agirre et al., 2012)
metrics: Fleiss' kappa (κ) for entailment inter-annotator agreement
Krippendorff's alpha (α) for relatedness inter-annotator agreement
5-point Likert scale relatedness score (average of ten human ratings) (descriptive)
limitations: Polish is under-resourced compared to English, so SICK's original procedure cannot be directly adapted.
Sentence transformation process is language-specific and must be defined from scratch for Polish.
Automatic transformations are based on imperfect dependency parsing, leading to errors that require human verification and correction.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dataset construction for compositional distributional semantics / semantic similarity and entailment

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with extensive experience designing and evaluating language resources and annotation pipelines for semantic tasks.
traits: Examines annotation protocol details (instructions, qualification, adjudication) and looks for potential sources of bias or systematic error
Values rigorous inter-annotator agreement reporting, appropriate statistical testing, and clear definitions of evaluation metrics
Checks reproducibility: expects dataset release, preprocessing code, transformation rules, and seed/sampling procedures to be documented
Focuses on dataset construction choices that affect model evaluation (pair generation, class balance, train/dev/test splits, and baseline/ablation setups)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Methodical reviewer who emphasizes experimental rigor and reproducibility; asks for clarifications, ablations, and additional analyses that validate the dataset's reliability for downstream evaluation.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Resources and Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in compositional distributional semantics and lexical semantics with a track record of building and using linguistic resources across languages.
traits: Assesses novelty relative to prior datasets (e.g., SICK and existing Polish corpora) and whether linguistic adaptations genuinely address language-specific phenomena
Values coverage of compositional phenomena (negation, quantifiers, argument alternations, morphology) and practical usefulness for CDS research
Considers broader impact: dataset accessibility, potential for cross-lingual work, benchmarking value, and ethical or licensing issues
Looks for evidence that the corpus will change researcher behavior (new baselines enabled, gaps filled) rather than being a near-duplicate translation of existing resources
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: High-level, impact-focused reviewer who weighs the work's novelty and usefulness for the community and asks for evidence that the dataset meaningfully advances research in compositional semantics.
